pageDesc: Compare bridge finality, trust assumptions, message paths, and failure modes without moving assets.
heading: Bridge Risk Simulator
subheading: Compare how different L2 and bridge routes depend on finality, validators, sequencers, liquidity, app configuration, and exit paths. This lab never asks you to bridge real assets.

native-optimistic.title: Native optimistic rollup bridge
native-optimistic.desc: Canonical bridge path with challenge-window finality and L1 contracts as the main trust anchor.

native-zk.title: Native ZK rollup bridge
native-zk.desc: Canonical bridge path where validity proofs move the state root or withdrawal proof forward.

external-validator.title: External validator bridge
external-validator.desc: Wrapped asset or message path secured by an external committee, oracle set, or multisig.

general-message.title: General message passing
general-message.desc: Arbitrary cross-chain messages delivered by configured oracle, relayer, or verification services.

liquidity-network.title: Liquidity network / fast bridge
liquidity-network.desc: Fast user fill from liquidity providers, with later settlement or canonical bridge backstop.

sequencer.degraded: Sequencer degraded or centralized outage risk

integration.linkout: Link to official bridge or route page
integration.embedded: Embedded bridge flow inside the app

bridgeNativeOptimisticDesc: Strong L1 anchoring, but withdrawals usually need challenge-window patience and a working exit path.
bridgeNativeZkDesc: Validity proofs can shorten finality, but prover, upgrade, data availability, and bridge contract risks still matter.
bridgeExternalValidatorDesc: Fast and flexible, but trust shifts toward an external verifier set and wrapped-asset accounting.
bridgeGeneralMessageDesc: Powerful for app-specific actions, but app configuration and replay protection become part of the safety boundary.
bridgeLiquidityDesc: User receives fast liquidity, while providers or solvers settle later through a bridge or dispute path.
modifierAmount: +{{points}} because the selected amount band increases blast radius.
modifierDirection: +{{points}} because this direction adds extra finality or message-shape complexity.
modifierSequencer: +{{points}} because sequencer degradation affects liveness and user timing.
modifierIntegration: +{{points}} because embedded flows make the app part of the risk surface.
finalityL1Confirm: Wait for source-chain confirmation before treating the route as started.
finalityChallengeWindow: Optimistic withdrawals may require a challenge window before L1 execution.
finalityExitExecution: A final claim or execution transaction may still be needed on the destination chain.
finalityValidityProof: Validity proof posting can shorten finality, but proof generation can still be delayed.
finalitySourceConfirm: The source-chain action must be finalized enough for the verifier model.
finalityValidatorAttest: External validators, oracles, or guardians attest that the source event happened.
finalityDestinationMint: Destination contracts mint, unlock, or execute after verifier acceptance.
finalityRelayerDelivery: A relayer or executor must deliver the message to the destination app.
finalityDestinationExecute: The destination app must execute with the expected calldata and replay protection.
finalityLiquidityFill: Liquidity provider fills the user quickly before canonical settlement finishes.
finalitySettlementLater: Provider settlement happens later and can fail or be disputed.
finalityCanonicalBackstop: A canonical bridge or dispute path should exist if the fast route fails.
trustL1Contracts: Canonical L1/L2 bridge contracts and their upgrade controls.
trustFraudProofs: Fraud-proof or challenge mechanisms and honest watcher availability.
trustUpgradeKeys: Governance, multisig, or security council upgrade keys.
trustValidityProofs: Validity proof system, verifier contract, prover availability, and DA assumptions.
trustExternalSet: External validator, guardian, oracle, or multisig set.
trustBridgeContracts: Bridge contracts, token wrappers, accounting, and pause controls.
trustOperationalKeys: Operational keys, signer rotation, emergency pause, and monitoring.
trustOracleRelayer: Oracle, relayer, DVN, or executor configuration chosen by the app.
trustAppConfig: Application-level chain IDs, endpoints, replay protection, and allowed routes.
trustLiquidityProvider: Liquidity provider or solver honesty and settlement incentives.
trustRouteSolvency: Route liquidity depth, solvency, and refund behavior.
messageLockMint: Asset route locks or burns on the source side, then mints or unlocks representation elsewhere.
messageCanonicalMessenger: Canonical messenger passes withdrawal or deposit messages through rollup contracts.
messageProofVerified: Destination execution waits for a proof or verified state update.
messageExternalAttestation: External verifier signs or attests to source-chain events.
messageArbitraryData: Arbitrary app calldata crosses chains and must be scoped carefully.
messageAppExecution: Destination app interprets the message and performs the final action.
messageAtomicSwap: Fast routes may use liquidity fills or swap-like settlement rather than arbitrary calls.
messageNoArbitraryCall: Many liquidity routes move assets only and do not safely carry arbitrary app instructions.
failureChallengeDelay: Withdrawal delay, watcher failure, or unresolved challenge can delay exits.
failureSequencerDowntime: Sequencer downtime can delay inclusion, withdrawals, or proof generation.
failureUpgradeRisk: Admin or governance upgrade keys can change bridge or rollup behavior.
failureProverDelay: Proof generation or verifier issues can delay finality.
failureValidatorCompromise: External validator compromise can unlock or mint assets incorrectly.
failureWrappedAsset: Wrapped assets add issuer, accounting, liquidity, and depeg risk.
failureContractBug: Bridge contract bugs can lock or drain assets.
failureMisconfiguredPath: Wrong app endpoint, chain ID, or trusted remote can send messages to the wrong logic.
failureRelayerDelay: Relayer or executor downtime can strand messages even if the source event is valid.
failureAppBug: Destination app bugs can mishandle a valid cross-chain message.
failureLiquidityShortage: Fast bridge liquidity can run out or quote a worse route under stress.
failurePriceSlippage: Fast fills can expose users to price, fee, or refund surprises.
failureRouteDispute: Solver or provider settlement can be disputed after the user-facing fill.
failureSequencerCurrentlyDegraded: Current sequencer degradation should be treated as live liveness risk.
failureIntegrationDependency: Embedded bridge UX makes your app responsible for warnings, route choice, and failure states.
checkOfficialBridge: Prefer official/canonical bridges for large or first-time movements.
checkWithdrawalDelay: Read the withdrawal delay and make sure the exit path is available.
checkSmallTest: Send a small test amount before moving meaningful value.
checkProofStatus: Check proof status, prover liveness, and destination claim steps.
checkValidatorSet: Review signer set, threshold, rotation policy, and public incident history.
checkBridgeHistory: Look for audits, exploit history, pause controls, and TVL concentration.
checkLimitAmount: Set a hard maximum amount for non-canonical routes.
checkMessageConfig: Verify chain IDs, endpoint addresses, trusted remotes, and app-level replay protection.
checkReplayProtection: Confirm the message cannot be replayed on another chain or app instance.
checkFallbackPath: Know who can retry, refund, or rescue a stuck message.
checkLiquidityDepth: Check route liquidity, quote freshness, fee, and refund behavior.
checkCanonicalBackstop: Confirm whether a canonical bridge can recover if the fast route fails.
boundaryNoAssets: The simulator never asks for a wallet, bridge transaction, private key, signature, or token approval.
boundaryNoRanking: Risk score is educational, not a bridge ranking, endorsement, or investment recommendation.
boundaryCredential: Credential design is documented separately; no NFT, DID, VC, or attestation system is described as live here.
